abstract:
Minority populations remain significantly underrepresented in clinical research, a disparity that is increasingly critical as cancer and chronic disease burdens escalate within these communities. Recruitment in the United States faces complex obstacles, most notably a pervasive lack of trust rooted in historical exploitation, cultural barriers, and socio-economic instability. To overcome these hurdles, we have implemented a recruitment model built on deep community engagement and the principles of global health research. Our core strategy involves hiring and training residents from the target communities to serve as research staff; as the first line of communication, they bridge the gap between investigators and participants through shared language, culture, and experience.

Applying this approach in the Texas Rio Grande Valley since 2001, we established a longitudinal cohort of over 5,000 Mexican Americans from randomly selected households. Our local team—initially recruited with basic training—has evolved into a high-capacity workforce proficient in advanced diagnostics, including ultrasound, FibroScan, retinal photography, and cognitive assessment. Today, this infrastructure supports several NIH-funded initiatives investigating liver disease, obesity, and diabetes. By integrating multi-omics with community-based trust, we are developing precision medicine frameworks that ensure chronic disease prevention and treatment are tailored to, and inclusive of, underserved populations.
